ks1.objective: Move safely, freeze when tagged, and learn a friend's name to free them.
successCriteria: Freezes on the spot when tagged
Says or asks a teammate's name

lks2.objective: Learn the names of group members and use them naturally while playing a tag game — building friendship through inclusion.
successCriteria: Knows most of the group's names by the end
Communicates clearly when freeing a teammate
Asks if they don't know — doesn't skip the frozen player

uks2.objective: Maintain dribble control while freeing teammates by name, using awareness and quick decision-making under pressure.
successCriteria: Keeps the ball under control while freeing others
Scans for frozen teammates and taggers simultaneously
Rotates who they free — no cliques

space.description: Square area sized to your group — roughly 20×20m for 15 children. Use cones to clearly define the boundary.

ks1.description: Smaller area (15×15m). Keep boundaries close so frozen players are quick to reach.

uks2.description: Larger area (25×20m). Add gates inside the area for progression 3+.

value.name: Friendship
value.text: Friendship begins with simple steps — learning someone's name and making them feel included. It's okay not to know a name at first, but it's your responsibility to ask. Encourage children to talk, ask questions and look out for teammates.

steps: Choose 2–3 taggers (bibs on). Everyone else are runners.
When tagged, the runner freezes on the spot.
To be freed: a teammate runs over, gives a high five, and says the frozen player's name.
If they don't know the name, they must ask before freeing.
Once freed, the player rejoins immediately.
Rotate taggers every 1–2 minutes so everyone has a turn.
progressions: Encourage children to free different teammates each round — build connections across the group.
Rotate the taggers regularly so all children experience both roles.
Introduce a football per player — they must dribble while tagging and freeing.
Challenge children to use their weaker foot when dribbling.
Add a condition: complete 5 toe taps before being freed.
questions: How does it feel when someone remembers your name?
How can you help someone feel included?
Why is it important to ask if you don't know someone's name?

step.space: Reduce area to make it harder for runners (taggers always close by)
step.task: Children must use specific parts of the foot when dribbling
step.equipment: Introduce gates that players must travel through before freeing teammates
step.people: Increase number of taggers to raise the challenge

base.showmanship: Highlight moments where children go out of their way to include others or help someone who is stuck. Make sure parents can hear the emphasis on communication and connection — name it out loud when you see it.

progressions: Start with just one tagger so the rules are clear.
Encourage frozen players to call out their own name to help.
questions: Whose name did you learn today?
How did you help someone get back into the game?
